Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Analyze the Request Input A scenario description involving a temporal profile model for an insurance claim handling process database schema PostgreSQL and specific anomalies in the temporal profile Task The LLM needs to act as an analyst It must Identify anomalies in the provided temporal profile model Generate hypotheses explaining these anomalies Propose SQL verification queries to investigate these anomalies using the provided schema claims adjusters claim_events Constraint Do not reference the instructions or explanations above Present the output independently as if it were a report or analysis based on the provided data Context PostgreSQL database

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Analyze the Data Anomalies Schema claims claim_id customer_id claim_amount claim_type submission_date adjusters adjuster_id name specialization region claim_events event_id claim_id activity R A E P N C timestamp resource additional_info Temporal Profile Key Points R A 1h avg 10m std Seems normal R E 1d avg 8h std Seems normal R P 25h avg 1h std Suspiciously low std dev implies rigid timing A C 2h avg 1h std Very fast closure skipping steps E N 5m avg 1m std Too fast might skip approval E C 1h avg 50m std Fast closure after eval P N 7 days avg 2 days std Huge delay bottleneck N C 30m avg 5m std Normal

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Focus Areas for Analysis R to P Receive to Approve Low variance suggests automation or batch processing rather than human review P to N Approve to Notify High variance and long duration suggests backlog or manual notification process A to C Assign to Close Short duration suggests claims closed without evaluation approval potential fraud or error E to N Evaluate to Notify Extremely short duration suggests approval step P might be skipped or automated instantly

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Section 2 Hypothesis Generation For R to P Automated approval for low risk claims Batch processing For P to N Manual notification process Email server issues Resource shortage For A to C Claims closed prematurely System error logging Close too early Fraudulent adjustment For E to N Missing Approve step in logs Auto approval after evaluation

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Section 3 SQL Verification Queries Need PostgreSQL syntax Need to join claim_events with itself self join to calculate time differences between activities for the same claim_id Need to join with claims or adjusters for context region type etc Query 1 Identify claims with A to C time less than threshold such as 4 hours to check for premature closure Query 2 Identify claims where P to N time greater than threshold such as 10 days to check for notification delays Query 3 Check for missing P events between E and N to verify skipped steps Query 4 Analyze R to P variance per adjuster or claim type to see if automation is selective

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Review against Constraints Do not reference the prompt instructions Present independently Ensure SQL is valid for PostgreSQL

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Final Polish Check SQL logic handling multiple events per activity per claim Correction In process mining we usually want the first instance of an activity for a specific claim to measure process flow I should add logic to select the minimum timestamp for each activity per claim

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Revised SQL Strategy Use a CTE to get the first timestamp for each activity per claim Then join the CTE to calculate deltas Query 1 A to C Find claims where Close happens very soon after Assign Query 2 P to N Find claims where Notify happens very late after Approve Query 3 E to N without P Find claims where Evaluate happens then Notify happens but no Approve event exists in between Query 4 R to P Variance Check standard deviation of R to P by region or adjuster

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Self Correction on SQL Make sure to handle cases where an activity might not exist LEFT JOIN For A to C if P is missing it is still an anomaly Self Correction on R to P The anomaly should be checked to see if this is consistent across all claim types or just specific ones

Name: Validation - PE
Text: PostgreSQL uses TIMESTAMP subtraction resulting in INTERVAL To get seconds use EXTRACT EPOCH FROM t2 minus t1 CTE first_events SELECT claim_id activity MIN timestamp as first_ts FROM claim_events GROUP BY claim_id activity Then join first_events to first_events on claim_id
